filer: National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners
overall_summary: NARUC files supplemental comments arguing that state commissions are best positioned to handle large load interconnection and cost allocation, and that federal action under the ANOPR is unnecessary and would impede the states. The filing cites six recent state proceedings (Virginia, Georgia, Ohio, Louisiana, Illinois, Michigan) as evidence that states are rapidly creating best practices that protect other ratepayers from cost shifts. NARUC contends there is no regulatory gap in states' ability to allocate transmission costs to large load customers, only an information gap that the states will close through coordination with FERC and RTOs.

quotes.text: The abundance of recent state undertakings demonstrate that state commissions are in the best position to ensure rational and efficient interconnections of new large loads while protecting all customers (including residential, commercial and large manufacturing customers) from improper cost-shifts or unfair interconnection processes.
quotes.concern: Argues states are best positioned to manage large load interconnection and protect customers from cost shifts

quotes.text: Federal regulation of large load interconnections will impede states’ abilities to (i) adapt quickly to industry
quotes.concern: Federal regulation would impede states' ability to adapt to a dynamic data center landscape

quotes.text: State commissions have the authority, regulatory tools, and unique expertise necessary to allocate transmission costs associated with large load interconnections directly to those end-use customers.  There is no regulatory gap.
quotes.concern: States already have authority to allocate transmission costs to large loads; no regulatory gap exists

quotes.text: NARUC also recognizes and supports the general conclusions of the Concentric Energy Advisors Whitepaper recently submitted into the record by WIRES, which highlights the potentially significant negative cost consequences to other retail customers of federal pre-emption of the large load interconnection process.
quotes.concern: Warns federal preemption would impose significant negative cost consequences on other retail customers

quotes.text: If FERC issues different federal rules for interconnection of data center loads, this may lead to increased costs or discriminatory procedures for other important end-use customers, such as Virginia manufacturers developing critical components
quotes.concern: Data-center-specific federal rules could raise costs or discriminate against other end-use customers

quotes.text: it remains unclear how cost allocation would work if some large loads have already paid for network upgrades through a federal interconnection process, making them exempt from other transmission costs at the retail level.  This could lead to costs being shifted to other retail ratepayers, while limiting the states’ ability to appropriately allocate costs and protect other customers.
quotes.concern: A federal upgrade-cost process could exempt large loads and shift transmission costs to other ratepayers

quotes.text: NARUC highlights six examples how the states are effectively handling large load customers and the surrounding issues:
quotes.concern: Cites six state proceedings as evidence states already handle large load issues effectively

quotes.text: this new large load tariff requires a minimum contract term of 14 years, a maximum ramp period of four years, minimum collateral requirements, and minimum demand charges based on contracted capacity.  All these protections will ensure that costs are not shifted to non-large load customers.
quotes.concern: Virginia's large load tariff uses contract terms, collateral, and demand charges to prevent cost shifts

quotes.text: These terms ensure large loads pay their share even if they use less power than planned and prevent potential stranded asset costs from being passed to other retail ratepayers.
quotes.concern: Ohio tariff terms make large loads pay their share and prevent stranded costs passing to ratepayers

quotes.text: This sliding-scale approach provides appropriate incentives for customers to submit realistic and supportable demand forecasts and helps mitigate the risk of speculative or inflated requests that could lead to inefficient planning or unnecessary system investment.
quotes.concern: Illinois deposit design discourages speculative interconnection requests and unnecessary system investment

quotes.text: All these examples demonstrate that states can be nimble in addressing large load interconnection issues.  By necessity, processes within RTOs, relying as they do on stakeholder consultation, are far less expeditious.
quotes.concern: States can act more quickly than RTO stakeholder processes on large load interconnection

quotes.text: changing the regulatory regime now will result in further uncertainty in the interconnection process and potentially unwind the state initiatives already put in place, such as those described above.
quotes.concern: Federal action now would create uncertainty and unwind existing state initiatives

quotes.text: no action in the ANOPR is necessary to enable state commission to cost-allocate transmission between end-use customers.
quotes.concern: No ANOPR action is needed for states to allocate transmission costs among end-use customers

bins.name: States over federal jurisdiction
bins.description: NARUC argues state commissions have the authority and expertise to manage large load interconnection and cost allocation, and that federal regulation under the ANOPR is unnecessary and would impede states' ability to adapt. It contends no ANOPR action is needed and that changing the regime now would create uncertainty and unwind state initiatives already in place.

bins.name: No gap in network upgrade allocation
bins.description: NARUC asserts states already have the tools to allocate transmission and network upgrade costs directly to large load end-use customers, so there is no regulatory gap. It warns that a federal upgrade-cost process could exempt large loads that paid through federal interconnection and shift costs to other retail ratepayers.

bins.name: Protecting other retail customers
bins.description: NARUC emphasizes shielding residential, commercial, and manufacturing customers from improper cost shifts and discriminatory procedures. It points to state tariff protections such as collateral, demand charges, and stranded-cost safeguards as evidence states already protect non-large-load customers.

bins.name: State deposit and collateral terms
bins.description: NARUC cites state-approved collateral, deposit, and sliding-scale fee structures, such as Virginia's collateral requirements and Illinois's study deposits, as effective tools that discourage speculative requests and protect ratepayers from stranded costs.

bins.name: States act faster than RTOs and FERC
bins.description: NARUC argues states can act more nimbly and expeditiously than RTO stakeholder processes or Section 206 FERC proceedings, which lack the statutory deadlines state commissions face.

bins.name: Cost allocation to large loads
bins.description: NARUC supports allocating large load interconnection and transmission costs to the large load customers themselves and preventing cost shifts to other ratepayers. It cites state tariffs using minimum demand charges, take-or-pay terms, exit fees, and collateral to achieve this, and warns federal preemption could impose negative cost consequences on other customers.
